channel.title: QE ArXiv Watch Weekly
channel.description: Weekly AI-synthesized digest of quality engineering research from arXiv. Covering Control Charts, Experimental Design, and Reliability Engineering.

item.title: QE ArXiv Watch: Week of May 25 - June 01, 2026
item.description:
Last week’s quiet theme: we’re getting better at aiming our statistical machinery. Not just “detect a change” or “learn the parameters,” but detect the right kind of change (serial dependence that breaks your chart) and learn the right things (only what the downstream decision actually cares about, under real constraints).

SPC: catching “your data stopped being i.i.d.” without assuming a distribution

Imagine your X̄ chart starts screaming, but nothing in the process changed—your sampling just got autocorrelated after a tooling tweak, a new batching routine, or a control loop retune. Weiß et al. go straight after that failure mode: monitor serial dependence itself, and do it distribution-free.

The trick is a clever replacement for ordinal-pattern (OP) monitoring. Instead of tracking patterns of ranks directly, they track “transcripts” between consecutive OPs, which can be mapped to Kendall distances. That gives three EWMA-style charts:

  • Δτ: chi-square-type distance on the transcript PMF
  • ΔK: chi-square-type distance on the Kendall-distance PMF
  • μK: a two-sided chart on the centered mean Kendall distance

Why you might care: these charts are built to be robust when the marginal distribution is unknown (continuous only) and when the usual i.i.d. assumption is the thing that’s drifting. Control limits are set by Monte Carlo to hit an in-control ARL≈370 (they calibrate under normality thanks to distribution-freeness), with practical smoothing choices (λ = 0.25, 0.10, 0.05).

Performance-wise, μK and ΔK look like the workhorses in many AR(1)-type situations—especially under strong negative autocorrelation, where classic tools can behave weirdly. In their chemical batch yield example (n=70, lag-1 ACF ≈ −0.588), the new charts flag early (around t≈23–25 for λ=0.05), dramatically earlier than a comparator that barely signals at all.

Caveat worth filing away: for a TEAR(1) setting, an OP-based β-chart still wins. So if your dependence is very “tail event” flavored, there may be specialized tools that beat the general-purpose transcript approach.

DOE: two strong signals—constraints are real, and decisions matter more than EIG

Constrained sequential design that actually plans ahead (COPEx)

Guo et al. tackle a problem you and I hit in the field constantly: sequential Bayesian design with hard feasibility constraints—can’t move the probe too far, can’t violate a remaining budget, can’t take the “perfect” next experiment because the factory won’t let you.

Their COPEx recipe is pragmatic: amortize the expensive bits offline (posterior surrogate + a proposal policy), then do online lookahead planning with a scenario tree for a short horizon (H≈2–3 seems to be the sweet spot). The payoff shows up most when constraints tighten: as step-size limits get smaller or budgets get stingier, non-myopic planning buys you more. Runtime grows quickly with horizon/branching (no free lunch), but they argue you don’t need deep lookahead to get most of the gain.

If you’re doing robotic inspection paths, adaptive metrology with move limits, or active learning with “travel cost,” this is one of the more deployment-shaped approaches we’ve seen.

Stop optimizing for information; optimize for the decision (GoBOED)

Go et al. lean into something many of us suspect: EIG is a nice proxy, but it’s not the objective. They propose GoBOED, which designs experiments to optimize robust downstream decision quality (mean objectives, chance constraints, CVaR), using an amortized posterior and a differentiable decision layer.

The practitioner-friendly insight is the “design window” effect. In their case studies, EIG picks a sharp, brittle optimum (e.g., “day 5”), while robust decision criteria produce a broad near-optimal window (e.g., “days 7–11”). That’s gold in real operations: if many designs are nearly equivalent for the decision, you can pick the one that’s easiest to run, cheapest, or least disruptive.

One question to take into next week

If we accept that (1) dependence is a first-class monitoring target and (2) designs should be judged by decision impact under constraints… how many of our current “optimal” procedures are optimal only in a world where data are i.i.d. and experiments are frictionless?

Featured Papers This Week

  • Weiß et al.: Using Transcripts for Nonparametric Monitoring of Serial Dependence (Control Charts)
  • Guo et al.: Constrained Bayesian Experimental Design via Online Planning (Experimental Design)
  • Go et al.: Goal-driven Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design for Robust Decision-Making Under Model Uncertainty (Experimental Design)

Explore More: Visit the QE ArXiv Watch Dashboard to browse all papers with AI summaries, interactive filtering, and paper chat.

This digest is automatically generated every Monday. Questions or feedback? Open an issue on our GitHub repository.
